_metadata.purpose: v15 catalog Step 2 — fill visitBasics/convenience/smartNotes/_poi_meta_extras for 24 attraction stops across 5 tours

field_format._poi_meta_extras: additional verified facts to merge into _poi_meta

changelog: v1.1 (2026-04-28): Added bomun_lake, daereungwon_tomb_complex, cheomseongdae for Step 3b cherry blossom tour.

v1.2 (2026-04-28): Added tongdosa_temple, wondong_plum_blossom_village for Step 3c plum+cherry tour. Wonhyoam Hermitage excluded — 8km mountain road approach + 900m elevation makes it unsuitable for day tours.

v1.3 (2026-04-28): Batch 1 - added 7 POIs from migrated Jeju tour enhancement: hallasan_eoseungsaengak, daepo_jusangjeolli_cliff, cheonjeyeon_falls, osulloc_tea_museum, aewol_cafe_street, jeongbang_falls, seongsan_ilchulbong. All facts verified from official sources (VisitKorea, Hallasan NP, AllTrails, Wikipedia, Trazy, Inside Jeju).

visitBasics.hours: Daily 04:30–19:20 (last admission 18:50)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free; private-car parking is paid
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 108 steps through pine grove, multiple seaside terraces and stairs

convenience.restroom: Available near main entrance and inside complex
convenience.parking: Haedong Yonggungsa Parking Lot (paid, per Visit Busan official)

smartNotes.photo: Sunrise rock at the seaward edge; stairs next to Daeungjeon Hall give the classic ocean-and-temple frame
smartNotes.facilities: Souvenir/snack stalls and small cafe at upper plaza; coastal trail leads further along the rocks
smartNotes.tip: Side doors for entering halls (front door reserved for monks); Buddha's Birthday (Apr–May) brings the lantern display

visitBasics.hours: Sky Capsule and Beach Train ~09:30–18:30 (extended to 20:30 in summer); Cheongsapo Daritdol Skywalk free 24h
visitBasics.closed: Operations weather-dependent; check the day of
visitBasics.admission: Free area; Sky Capsule 40,000 KRW (2 ppl) / 45,000 (3) / 50,000 (4) one-way; Beach Train ticketed separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy along the wooden Green Railway boardwalk (Mipo↔Cheongsapo 2.3 km, ~30 min); flat at stations

convenience.restroom: Available at Mipo, Cheongsapo and Songjeong stations
convenience.parking: Public lots near Mipo Station and Cheongsapo Station (paid)

cheongsapo_blue_line_park.photo_spot_pin: Cheongsapo Station crosswalk (Slam Dunk look-alike) and Daritdol Skywalk glass deck

smartNotes.photo: Cheongsapo Station crosswalk for the famous Slam Dunk-style frame; Daritdol Skywalk for glass-bottom sea view
smartNotes.facilities: Ticketing counters at Mipo, Cheongsapo and Songjeong only; convenience store and food at Mipo plaza
smartNotes.tip: Sky Capsule books out fast at sunset slots — reserve in advance via Klook or the official site; Mipo→Cheongsapo direction has the better light at sunset

visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat grass paths and paved walkways across 14 ha of grounds

convenience.restroom: Available at Memorial Service Hall and visitor center
convenience.parking: On-site lot — free for first 90 min, then ₩1,200/hr

smartNotes.photo: Wall of Remembrance (40,896 names on 140 marble panels) and the Symbolic Area where the 22 sending-state flags fly
smartNotes.facilities: Memorial Service Hall, Memorabilia Hall, UN Forces Monument, and adjacent UN Peace Memorial Hall
smartNotes.tip: Watch the 12-minute documentary in Memorial Service Hall first (English available); dress neatly, keep voices low, and no food on the grounds

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (Mar–Oct) / 09:00–17:00 (Nov–Feb); some shops/cafes keep their own hours
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (a living residential community)
visitBasics.admission: Free village entry; optional Stamp Map ≈ 2,000 KRW
visitBasics.walking: Strenuous — very steep alleys and stairs; not stroller- or wheelchair-friendly in many sections

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at the Tourist Information Center and around the main viewpoints
convenience.parking: Gamcheon Public Parking Lot 2 (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Little Prince and Fox bench (long queue) and Haneul Maru Observatory for the sweeping rainbow-roof and bay view
smartNotes.facilities: Hanbok rental, craft workshops, Korean candy stalls, cafes; small village museum at the entrance
smartNotes.tip: Residents live here — keep voices down, no drones, take rubbish with you; wear grippy shoes for the steep lanes

visitBasics.hours: Daily 05:00–22:00 (vendor hours vary; live-fish section officially 06:00–22:00)
visitBasics.closed: 1st and 3rd Tuesdays of every month
visitBasics.admission: Free entry; pay-as-you-go for seafood and 2F cooking-fee
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — ground floor flat (wet, can be slippery); stairs to 2F sashimi/restaurant level

convenience.restroom: Available inside the main building on each floor
convenience.parking: Basement lot at the main building, ₩700 / 10 min

smartNotes.photo: Outdoor pier behind the building for the harbor-and-fishing-boat shot; Jagalchi ajummas at the live-fish stalls
smartNotes.facilities: 1F live seafood, 2F sashimi & restaurants, dried-fish section, rooftop Sky Park (when open), Sashimi Center buffet on 5F
smartNotes.tip: Buy the fish on 1F, then bring it upstairs to a 2F restaurant and pay a small cooking fee — that's the local way; address 52 Jagalchihaean-ro, Jung-gu

visitBasics.hours: Park 04:00–24:00 (Mar–Oct) / 05:00–24:00 (Nov–Feb); facilities close ~20:00
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; Danubi Train and lighthouse close in inclement weather
visitBasics.admission: Park free; Danubi Train 4,000 KRW round-trip / 2,000 one-way (adult), 2,000/1,500 youth/child
visitBasics.walking: Strenuous if walking the full 4.3 km loop; lighthouse area has many steep stairs (no elevator)

convenience.restroom: Available at the main entrance, Observatory, and lighthouse plaza
convenience.parking: Taejongdae Park Parking Lot (paid; vehicles enter from 09:00)

smartNotes.photo: Yeongdo Lighthouse cliff edge and the Observatory overlook toward Oryukdo and (on clear days) Tsushima
smartNotes.facilities: Tourist information center at the entrance, Danubi Train ticket booth, food/snack stalls at the rocks below the lighthouse
smartNotes.tip: Buy Danubi Train tickets in advance during high season — your ticket is tied to a specific time slot; use the Visit Busan City Tour Bus wristband for 25% off the train fare

visitBasics.hours: Park 24h year-round; Busan Diamond Tower observatory 10:00–22:00 (last admission 21:30)
visitBasics.closed: Park open year-round; Tower may close in severe weather
visitBasics.admission: Park free; Busan Diamond Tower 12,000 KRW (adult) / 9,000 KRW (senior 65+ / child 3–12) / under-3 free
visitBasics.walking: Easy — escalator from Gwangbok-ro / Nampo-dong handles the climb; gentle paths within the park

convenience.restroom: Available within the park and inside the Tower lobby
convenience.parking: No dedicated lot at the park; nearby Lotte Department Store Gwangbok lot (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Tower observatory windows facing Busan Port at golden hour; flower clock and Statue of Admiral Yi Sun-sin in the park
smartNotes.facilities: Souvenir shops, cafe, AR/media zones inside the Tower; bell pavilion and dragon sculpture in the park
smartNotes.tip: Climb via the outdoor escalator from Gwangbok-ro Fashion Street rather than the stairs; Tower pairs well with Nampo-dong dinner — visit at sunset for day-and-night views

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (last entry 17:00)
visitBasics.closed: Mondays (some sources); open during major Korean holidays
visitBasics.admission: Adults 8,000 KRW / Under 18 5,000 KRW (no senior or group discount — private property)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — gentle slopes, mostly flat, ~1–2 hour loop on natural unpaved trails

convenience.restroom: Available near the entrance/ticket booth
convenience.parking: Free spacious lot at the entrance (closes after 18:00)

smartNotes.photo: Gutteo Bamboo Grove ('The King: Eternal Monarch' filming site) and the second bamboo forest with sunlight filtering through the canopy
smartNotes.facilities: Free bamboo walking-stick rental and mosquito spray at the gate; no on-site cafe — bring water
smartNotes.tip: Pets, drones, alcohol, smoking, bicycles and music speakers are all prohibited (working forest with wildlife); plant/insect collection is forbidden

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (last admission 17:00)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free (since 2023, per the revised Cultural Heritage Protection Act)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — stone steps, slopes and gravel paths; comfortable shoes recommended

convenience.restroom: Free public restrooms (designed in mini-temple style) inside the complex
convenience.parking: On-site lot (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Cheongungyo / Baegungyo bridge stairway (NT 23) below Jahamun Gate; Dabotap (NT 20) and Seokgatap (NT 21) pagodas in the main courtyard
smartNotes.facilities: Tourist Information Center at the entrance; Bulguksa Museum (separate ticket 2,000 / 1,000 KRW, closed Mondays)
smartNotes.tip: Modest dress; remove shoes when entering the halls; no drones; do not photograph monks or worshippers (signs marked clearly)

visitBasics.hours: Weekdays 10:00–18:00 / Weekends & holidays 10:00–19:00 (last admission 30 min before close); extended Saturday & last-Wednesday hours seasonally
visitBasics.closed: New Year's Day, Seollal, Chuseok, first Monday of March and November
visitBasics.admission: Free (special exhibitions may require advance reservation)
visitBasics.walking: Easy — multiple flat galleries plus a large outdoor exhibition area

convenience.restroom: Available in every wing and outdoor exhibit area
convenience.parking: Free on-site lot

smartNotes.photo: Sacred Bell of King Seongdeok (NT 29) at the front of the museum; outdoor stone-pagoda field at sunset
smartNotes.facilities: Silla History Gallery, Silla Art Gallery, Wolji Gallery (Anapji Pond relics), Children's Museum, English audio guide, museum shop, cafe
smartNotes.tip: Free English museum tour on Sundays at 10:00 and 14:00 (90 min, departs from 1F Silla History Gallery); the Cheonma-zuka Golden Crown (NT 188) is the must-see in the Silla History Gallery

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor village 24h; shops and the Choi Family House typically 10:00–17:00 (some take a 15:00–18:00 lunch break)
visitBasics.closed: Village open year-round; Choi Family House may close on Mondays
visitBasics.admission: Free village entry; some workshops and the Gyeongju Choi Wealthy Family House may charge a small fee
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat, stone-paved lanes; comfortable shoes recommended

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms near the village entrance and main plaza
convenience.parking: On-site lot near the village (free, fills quickly on weekends)

smartNotes.photo: Tiled-roof rooflines along the stone-walled lanes and the Choi Family House courtyard
smartNotes.facilities: Hanbok rental, Gyodong Beopju traditional liquor shop (Important Intangible Cultural Property No. 86-3), local eateries selling Gyochon makgeolli, ssambap and injeolmi ice cream
smartNotes.tip: Pair with Woljeonggyo Bridge across the stream; weekday visits are noticeably quieter than weekends, and many shops close around 17:00

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor bridge 24h; pavilion exhibition hall typically 09:00–18:00
visitBasics.closed: Bridge open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat, wide wooden bridge deck; pavilion stairs at each end

convenience.restroom: Public restroom on the Gyochon side of the bridge
convenience.parking: Use Gyochon Hanok Village parking (a 2-min walk away)

smartNotes.photo: Reflection of the illuminated bridge on Namcheon Stream after sunset; the pavilion's traditional dancheong at golden hour
smartNotes.facilities: Inside one pavilion is a small free exhibition on the bridge's history and Silla-era engineering
smartNotes.tip: Reconstructed in 2018 from records of the original 760 AD Silla bridge — best visited at dusk to catch both the daylight architecture and the night illumination (lit ~sunset to about 22:00)

visitBasics.hours: Mar–May & Sep–Oct: 09:00–18:00; Jun–Aug: 09:00–18:30; Nov–Feb: 09:00–17:00 (last admission 1 hour before close)
visitBasics.closed: Tuesdays
visitBasics.admission: Adult 3,000 KRW / Youth (7–18) 1,500 KRW; free for ≤6, seniors 65+, hanbok wearers
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — large palace grounds (1–2 hours of walking); paths are gravel and stone

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms throughout the palace grounds
convenience.parking: Limited on-site paid lot; nearby public lots near Gwanghwamun

smartNotes.photo: Royal Guard Changing Ceremony at Gwanghwamun (10:00 and 14:00, ~20 min, free; cancelled on Tuesdays and in heavy rain); Gyeonghoeru Pavilion reflected in its pond
smartNotes.facilities: Free guided tours in English (11:00, 13:30, 15:30); hanbok rental shops surround the palace gates; National Folk Museum and Children's Museum on the grounds
smartNotes.tip: Wearing hanbok gets you in for free — many rental shops offer a 1–4 hour package; arrive 10–15 min before the 10:00 ceremony for a clear view

visitBasics.hours: Open 24 hours (outdoor); resident-quiet hours 22:00–10:00 enforced
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (residential — please be respectful)
visitBasics.admission: Free; some hanok museums charge separately (≈ 3,000 KRW)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — uphill cobblestone alleys; not stroller-friendly in places

convenience.restroom: Bukchon Cultural Center and Bukchon Tourist Information Center provide public restrooms
convenience.parking: No dedicated lot — use Anguk Station-area paid lots and walk in

smartNotes.photo: Gahoe-dong Alley 8 (downhill view toward N Seoul Tower over hanok roofs) and Alley 31 — the two iconic 'Bukchon 8 Views' angles
smartNotes.facilities: Bukchon Cultural Center (free programs), small hanok cafes and craft workshops; map and route info at the visitor center
smartNotes.tip: This is a residential neighborhood — keep voices down, do not enter private gates, and respect the marshals on Gahoe-dong Alley 11; weekday mornings are quietest

visitBasics.hours: Food alley daily 09:00–23:00 (most stalls 11:00 onward); general market 09:00–18:00 (closed Sun); apparel 10:00–19:00 (closed Sun)
visitBasics.closed: Many food stalls close on Sundays or Mondays — varies by stall
visitBasics.admission: Free entry; per-meal budget ≈ 8,000–15,000 KRW
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat covered alleys; can be very crowded at lunch and dinner

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at market entrances
convenience.parking: Use Jongno 4-ga Jongmyo Public Parking Lot (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Bindae-tteok (mung bean pancake) stalls being fried on cast-iron griddles; Mayak Gimbap rolls at Mother & Daughter's Gimbap
smartNotes.facilities: Hundreds of food stalls, sit-down restaurants on the outer ring, hanbok and vintage clothing on the 2F
smartNotes.tip: Cash is still preferred at smaller stalls; lunch rush 12:00–14:00 means long queues — try arriving 11:00 or after 14:30; Netflix-famous knife-cut noodles at Cho Yonsoon's stall

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor street 24h; shops typically 10:00–22:00; pedestrian-only on Saturdays 14:00–22:00 and Sundays 10:00–22:00
visitBasics.closed: Most shops open year-round; some close on Mondays
visitBasics.admission: Free; some museums and craft workshops charge separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy — wide, mostly flat, pedestrian-friendly main street with branching alleys

convenience.restroom: Available inside Anyoung Insadong, Ssamziegil, and at Anguk Station Exit 6
convenience.parking: Use nearby paid lots (Insa-dong is largely pedestrian); Anguk Station has metered street parking

smartNotes.photo: Ssamziegil's spiraling indoor courtyard and the calligraphy-style Hangul shopfronts along Insadong-gil
smartNotes.facilities: Roughly 40% of Korea's antique shops, plus traditional tea houses, hanji paper shops, calligraphy stores, art galleries, and the Korean Craft and Design Foundation Gallery
smartNotes.tip: Branch into the side alleys for the quieter tea houses and locally-run craft shops — the main street is the touristy part; weekend afternoons are pedestrian-only and most lively but also the most crowded

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor 24h year-round; cherry blossom illumination ~sunset to 22:00 in early April
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; some lakeside cafes close Mondays
visitBasics.admission: Free (lake and walking trail); paddle boats and bike rentals priced separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat 8km paved loop trail along the lakeside; can be sampled in 1–2 km segments without doing the full loop

convenience.restroom: Available at the Tourist Information Center, Bomun Pavilion, and major resort access points along the loop
convenience.parking: Multiple free public lots around the resort complex (Hoban Square, Bomun Pavilion); fills quickly during the festival weekend

smartNotes.photo: Bomun Pavilion (CNN '50 most beautiful places in Korea') and the wooden bridge for reflection shots; sunset around the east shore near Gyeongju World
smartNotes.facilities: Bicycle and tandem rentals (~₩10,000/3hr), paddle boats, lakeside cafes and restaurants, Bomun Open Air Stage with free traditional music performances Apr–Jan
smartNotes.tip: Cherry blossom peak is typically late March to early April (~7-day window); arrive on a weekday morning or after 16:00 to avoid the worst weekend crowds — full loop walk takes ~2 hours, but most tours sample only 1–2 km

visitBasics.hours: Park 09:00–22:00 (until 21:00 in winter); Cheonmachong Tomb interior 09:00–22:00
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Park: Free. Cheonmachong Tomb interior (NT-class artifacts): Adult 3,000 KRW / Teenager 2,000 / Child 1,000
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat grassy paths between the burial mounds; spread across 126,500 m² (good for stroller and senior visitors)

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at the main entrance and near Cheonmachong Tomb
convenience.parking: Daereungwon parking lot at the main gate (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Daereungwon Stone Wall Road (cherry blossom tunnel — annual festival Apr 3–5); the giant magnolia tree behind Hwangnamdaechong; Tomb of King Michu surrounded by cherry trees
smartNotes.facilities: Small museum inside Cheonmachong Tomb showing 11,526 excavated artifacts including the famous Heavenly Horse painting (the only known Silla-era painting); Day Pass option available (₩4,000–8,000 covering Daereungwon + Donggung & Wolji Pond)
smartNotes.tip: Climbing the burial mounds is prohibited (heavy fine); 30–60 min is typical for a walk-through, longer if you enter Cheonmachong; pairs naturally with Cheomseongdae and Woljeonggyo Bridge — all within a 3 km radius

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor viewing 24h; cherry blossom illumination ~sunset to 22:00 in early April
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (it is a stone monument in a public field — no admission gate)
visitBasics.admission: Free
visitBasics.walking: Very easy — flat open field; closest stop on the Gyeongju downtown loop

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at the adjacent visitor center / Donggung area
convenience.parking: Cheomseongdae parking lot (paid) and shared parking with the wider Gyeongju Historic Area

smartNotes.photo: Cheomseongdae itself with cherry blossoms or canola flowers in the surrounding field; the Gyerim Forest immediately south is also photogenic
smartNotes.facilities: Open field with seasonal flower plantings (cherry blossoms in spring, cosmos in autumn); food trucks during festivals; English info plaques at base of the tower
smartNotes.tip: The 27-tier stone tower from 647 AD is the oldest astronomical observatory in East Asia and a National Treasure — counting the stones (365 to match days of the year) is a fun activity for kids; only 5–15 min needed for the stop itself

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor temple grounds 24h; Seongbo Museum 09:00–17:00; Daeungjeon main hall typically open daylight hours
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; Seongbo Museum closed Mondays
visitBasics.admission: Temple grounds: Free (most general visit areas); Seongbo Museum (first Buddhist museum in Korea, 600+ paintings, 30,000 artifacts): typically ₩2,000–3,000 adult — confirm at entrance
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 1.2 km riverside paved trail from parking lot to main temple entrance (flat, can go barefoot per VisitKorea), then temple courtyards spread across hillside slopes (some stairs and gentle inclines)

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at the parking lot, Seongbo Museum entrance, and inside the main temple compound near Daeungjeon
convenience.parking: Two large paid parking lots near the trailhead (Yangsan City + Tongdosa joint operation); fills during festivals (plum blossom, Buddha's Birthday lantern festival)

smartNotes.photo: Jajangmae (지장매) — the 350-year-old red plum tree planted by Master Jajang, peak ~Late February to Early March, 'first messenger of spring'; Daeungjeon (National Treasure #290 — unique because it has no Buddha image inside, instead the Diamond Stairway/Geumgang Gyedan enshrines actual Buddha relics behind it); the riverside pine forest trail (curved 'dragon-branch' red pines, Iljumun gate approach)
smartNotes.facilities: Templestay program available in English (lotus lanterns, tea ceremony, meditation, 108 prostrations); Seongbo Museum (largest Buddhist museum in Korea); 17 hermitages on the mountain (most require additional hiking — not part of standard temple visit)
smartNotes.tip: UNESCO World Heritage 'Sansa, Buddhist Mountain Monasteries in Korea' (2018) — one of Korea's Three Jewel Temples (Bulbo Temple, enshrining genuine Buddha relics). Daeungjeon famously contains NO Buddha image — only an altar facing the Diamond Stairway. Best visit time is early morning before tour buses arrive (08:30–10:00) or late afternoon (15:00–16:30) for golden light on the temple

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor village/festival grounds: open 24h during bloom (~late Feb to mid Mar); festival main events typically Fri–Sun in early March
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (the village itself); plum blossom peak window only ~10 days mid-March
visitBasics.admission: Free — the village and trails are open to the public; festival booth purchases are pay-direct
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat farm paths along the Nakdong River + railway tracks; can be sampled in 1–2 km segments without committing to a long walk

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at Wondong Station (railway), the festival main stage area, and the visitor center during festival weekends
convenience.parking: Free public lots near Wondong Station; fills heavily on festival weekend mornings (insider tip: arrive before 09:00 or after 15:00)

smartNotes.photo: The signature shot is plum blossom branches framing the Nakdong River + the railway tracks — passing trains create a once-per-30-minutes photo moment; festival weekends add traditional music busking under the trees
smartNotes.facilities: Wondong Plum Blossom Festival (typically March 1–3 + 10-day extended bloom window); 'Plum Noodle Market' selling locally made plum-infused noodles, plum extracts, plum highball, Maesil products; 'Minari Festival' running concurrently with greenhouse pop-up restaurants serving Minari Samgyeopsal — the area's signature spring dish (clean-water parsley grilled with pork belly, only available at this season)
smartNotes.tip: Yangsan Wondong is 'romantic and nostalgic' (Korean tourism tagline) compared to Gwangyang's grand mountain-scale plum festival — smaller, less-crowded, with a stronger railway/river aesthetic. Pairs naturally with Tongdosa Temple (~25 min drive) for a Yangsan-focused half day.

_poi_meta.historical_feature: Tochika — 1945 Imperial Japanese military pillbox/communication post at summit

visitBasics.hours: Trail accessible roughly 05:00–18:00 (varies seasonally; longer in summer May–Aug)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; closures possible in extreme weather
visitBasics.admission: Free; no entrance fee, no reservation required (only Seongpanak/Gwaneumsa summit trails require advance QR reservation)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 1.3 km one-way, ~200 m elevation gain via wooden stairs and boardwalks; 30–40 min up, plan 1–1.5 hr round trip

convenience.restroom: Available at Eorimok Visitor Center near the trailhead
convenience.parking: Eorimok Trail parking lot at the visitor center (free)

smartNotes.photo: Summit observation deck for 360° view of Jeju City, the northern coast and the higher Hallasan peaks (Baengnokdam crater on clear days). Tochika ruins for a quick history shot
smartNotes.facilities: Visitor Center with information panels and restrooms at trailhead; benches and rest points along the trail; no shops or vending past the entrance
smartNotes.tip: Wooden stairs are slippery after rain and icy in winter (Dec–Feb) — micro-spikes recommended in winter. Pets and drones are not allowed in Hallasan National Park

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (last admission ~17:00; hours may shift seasonally with sunset)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Adults ₩2,000 / Youth (7–24) ₩1,000 / Free for under 7 and over 65; group rate (10+) ₩1,600 adult / ₩600 youth
visitBasics.walking: Easy — 0.8 km loop on paved walkways and wooden viewing decks; ~12–15 min walk, fully flat

convenience.restroom: Available at the entrance plaza and visitor area
convenience.parking: Paid parking lot adjacent to entrance — ₩1,000 small / ₩2,000 medium (sedan to 15-seater) / ₩3,000 large vehicles

smartNotes.photo: First viewing deck for the classic hexagonal-columns frame; far deck for waves crashing into the columns (best at high tide — waves can shoot 20+ m up the cliff)
smartNotes.facilities: Souvenir kiosks at entrance selling Hallabong (Jeju tangerine) juice in dol-hareubang shaped bottles, hot dogs, octopus skewers, ice cream and accessories; ICC Jeju (Convention Center) and Yakcheonsa Temple are walking distance
smartNotes.tip: Plan ~30–45 min total. The wooden deck doesn't allow descent to the columns themselves — they are viewed from above. Best photos when sunlight hits at an angle (mid-morning or late afternoon); spray reaches the deck at high tide so cameras may need protection

_poi_meta.name_meaning: Pond of the Emperor of Heaven

visitBasics.hours: Mar–Oct 08:00–18:30; Nov–Feb 09:00–17:30. Last admission 1 hour before closing
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Adults ₩2,500 / Youth & Children ₩1,350 (covers larger valley and Seonimgyo Bridge)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — many stairs across the three tiers; total ~1–1.5 km loop with significant ascent/descent. Plan 1–1.5 hr

convenience.restroom: Available near the ticket office at the entrance (none past the bridge)
convenience.parking: On-site parking lot — free for visitors

smartNotes.photo: Seonimgyo Bridge (vermilion arch with seven nymphs carved on the trusses) for the classic shot; viewing deck for the second tier — the most active and photogenic waterfall (30 m drop into emerald pool); Cheonjeru Pavilion deck for distant overview
smartNotes.facilities: Benches and shaded rest spots along the path; no shops past the bridge — pack water in summer; Chilseonyeo (Seven Nymphs) Festival held here in May of even-numbered years
smartNotes.tip: First tier ONLY flows after heavy rain (often dry in winter); the second tier is the reliable attraction. Lots of stairs — comfortable shoes required. Note: do NOT confuse with Cheonjiyeon Falls (different waterfall in Seogwipo old town). Swimming prohibited

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–19:00 (Apr–Oct) / 09:00–18:00 (Nov–Mar); subject to change in adverse weather
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free entry to museum and tea fields; tea, desserts, classes priced separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy — paved paths and ramps throughout; tea fields fully flat. Stroller- and wheelchair-friendly

convenience.restroom: Available throughout the museum and adjacent Innisfree Jeju House
convenience.parking: Large free parking lot on-site

smartNotes.photo: The teacup-shaped main building exterior; 2nd-floor observatory window over the Seogwang tea fields; the row of giant teacup planters at the field entrance; Innisfree Jeju House garden path with blue flowers in season
smartNotes.facilities: Cafe (matcha ice cream, green tea swiss roll, Hallabong/tangerine tea, Camellia flower tea); flagship gift shop; Tea Stone (since 2013) — paid tea brewing classes; adjacent Innisfree Jeju House (sister Amorepacific brand) with handmade soap workshop (~₩20,000) and Green Cafe brunch
smartNotes.tip: Arrive at opening (09:00) on weekdays — tour buses crowd the place from 10:00 onward. Green tea ice cream queue gets long midday. Pair with Innisfree Jeju House across the path for a 1.5–2 hr visit. Combo well with Sanbangsan (~25 min) or Hallim Park (~30 min)

_poi_meta.promenade_named_after: Jang Han Chul (Jeju-born Joseon-era writer on Korean maritime history)

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor area 24h; cafes typically 09:00–22:00 (vary by venue)
visitBasics.closed: Outdoor area open year-round; individual cafes have their own days off
visitBasics.admission: Free area; food & drinks priced individually (~₩6,000–12,000 per cafe order)
visitBasics.walking: Easy — paved coastal promenade between Gwakji Beach and Handam Village (~2 km, 20–30 min one way)

convenience.restroom: Inside cafes and at small public facilities along the coastal trail
convenience.parking: Limited — small public lots near the main cafes; some venues offer free private parking for patrons. Roads are narrow, parking can be challenging on weekends

smartNotes.photo: West-facing coastal cliffs at sunset (one of the best sunset spots on Jeju); the small cove at Handam for transparent kayaks; iconic Bomnal Cafe terrace seafront; Gwakji Beach 'jelly sea' shallows in summer
smartNotes.facilities: Dozens of cafes, bakeries (Cafe Knotted, Randy's Donuts), seafood ramyeon at Nolman (₩10,000, single dish), burger shops; transparent kayak rentals at Handam (~₩10,000/30 min, 2-person boats with life jackets)
smartNotes.tip: Sunset is the headline — west-facing coast catches the sun dropping into the sea. Weekday afternoons quieter; weekend afternoons can be packed and parking near impossible. Bus 202/100/200 from Jeju City stops at Handam-dong

_poi_meta.unique_feature: Only waterfall in Asia falling directly into the ocean
_poi_meta.legend_inscription: Seobulgwacha — origin of the Seogwipo place-name (Seobul Headed Back West)

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–17:40 (last admission 30 min before closing; slight seasonal variation)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (occasional closures for high tide / typhoon)
visitBasics.admission: Adults ₩2,000 / Youth (7–24) ₩1,000; free under 7 and 65+
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — ~130 stairs down to the base, then unmarked rocky terrain to reach the waterfall. Stairs slippery when wet; rocks slippery and irregular — sturdy shoes essential

convenience.restroom: At the entrance/ticketing area before the descent (none at the base)
convenience.parking: On-site parking lot at the entrance

smartNotes.photo: Mid-descent platform for the full-cliff waterfall + ocean frame; rock-hopping at the base for close-up shots with the ocean spray (be cautious — slippery); the cliff-top observatory for distant view of Seopseom and Munseom Islands
smartNotes.facilities: Souvenir/snack stalls and ramyeon shops near the entrance; Haenyeo (women diver) ajumma stalls at the base selling fresh sea urchin and conch sashimi (seasonal); Sojeongbang Waterfall ~300 m east; Seogwipo Maeil Olle Market 12 min walk
smartNotes.tip: Best flow after heavy rain or in spring (Hallasan snowmelt) — flow can be light in summer dry spells. AVOID flip-flops on the rock terrain. Watch tide — at high tide many rocks at the base are submerged. Best light: mid-morning (10:00) and late afternoon (15:00–17:00)

visitBasics.hours: May–Aug 04:30–20:00; Mar–Apr & Sep–Oct 05:00–19:00; Nov–Feb 06:00–18:00. Last ticketing 1 hr before closing
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; opens 1 hr before sunrise to allow first-light viewing
visitBasics.admission: Adults ₩2,000 / Youth (7–24) ₩1,000; free for under 7 and 65+; group (10+) ₩1,600 adult / ₩800 youth
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 0.8 km one-way trail, ~140 m elevation gain via wooden deck staircase (300–500 steps); summit climb 20–30 min, full round trip 45–60 min. One-way path prevents head-on collisions

convenience.restroom: Available at the entrance and at rest points along the trail
convenience.parking: Large free parking lot at the entrance plaza

smartNotes.photo: Summit deck for the bowl-shaped crater shot; mid-trail viewpoint for the dramatic coastline + Udo Island frame; the 99-rock 'crown' rim seen from the base looking up; haenyeo divers at the south coast cove (Udu Beach side)
smartNotes.facilities: Daily haenyeo (women diver) free-diving demonstrations at 13:30 and 15:00 at the south coast cove (singing performance + live diving for shellfish — they sell their fresh catch sliced as sashimi after the show); food/drink stalls at the entrance; Udo Island ferry from Seongsan Port (~5 min away)
smartNotes.tip: TRIPLE UNESCO designation (World Natural Heritage 2007 + Biosphere Reserve + Global Geopark). For the iconic sunrise, arrive 30 min before sunrise time (gates open 1 hr before sunrise). Wooden staircase but stairs throughout — not stroller- or wheelchair-friendly. Combine with Udo Island ferry (~10 min crossing) or Seopjikoji (~15 min drive south) to make a half-day in east Jeju
